speaker: interviewer
text: So this is The Survivors of the Shoah interview with Itka Zygmuntowicz, March 3rd, 1996, tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I mentioned to you, when they came in, I was in the house when they started to interrogate my mother. And then when they took her away, I was there-- so many horrible things happening to other Jews. So I knew, and I was afraid that I might not see my mother. So I followed her. At first they chased me away, then one got a bright idea, said-- to take me, too. And I went, and I figured no matter what's going to happen, I love my mother so much. We had such a close relationship. And I said, whatever happen, I have to be with her. So we went. They took us to the Gestapo station. When we came there, they separated us. I was sitting, and they took her in a room. To this, I'm only ear-witness, because I didn't see what they did. But what I heard were terrible screams. I've never heard anybody scream like this. So I realized that they must be torturing her. They wanted to her to submit-- not to resist-- and to tell them-- to betray another fellow Jew. And my mother was determined not to. And after a long time screaming and screaming and screaming that was tearing my heart, became completely silent. And that's when my heart almost stopped beating. Because while she was screaming, at least I had a sign that she's alive. But after it got silent, I no longer knew what my mother still alive or not. After what looked to me like an eternity, but actuality wasn't that long, the door swung open. And two Gestapos-- one was huddled to carry under the armpit, the other under the other armpit, dragging her across-- across the floor. She was beaten horribly. And as they took her out, they took me in that she should see. And I didn't know if she's still conscious or not. And I looked at her and just with her face she gave me sign not to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that moment, I couldn't understand. I was too young. Why would she allow herself to be so beaten, and even maybe killed, to protect a stranger practically?

speaker: interviewee
text: It- it was just an acquaintance. We just knew who he is, because I went to school there. And now, too, they take me in. She's going to let them beat me. I couldn't understand, may she forgive me. And but I knew that I have to-- that this is an important moment. And I wasn't completely dumb. And I understood-- started to realize that maybe she must have a good reason. So they took me in, and I was next in line in beating. From what I later found out what they beat my mother, though it was severe, was nothing in comparison how they beat her. And I didn't say. I, too, was silent. I, too, would not betray somebody else. Because it's not menschlich. And that was my test, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't say even after I was beaten. And they saw that neither my mother, even though she heard me screaming, she didn't tell. And I didn't tell. So I heard one of them saying [SPEAKING GERMAN], that he doesn't believe that we know. And they let us go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I realized then that that was my mother's and my test of menschlichkeit. And that was the first time I learned about spiritual resistance, that you don't have to take just a gun, that there's something else. And this story affected me very much, affected me very much. Anyhow, the religious Jews suffered much, far greater. We all suffered, because the rations, everyday was-- there was killing and shootings, and beatings, and you know all those things, and forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of the things you saw on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw one time, you know, some religious Jews who had beards would put on a kerchief, so they would hide their beard. Because I remember the first time when I saw they were pulling the hairs one by one. Another time I saw that they were-- they cut just a half, and they leave the others just to mock us. Because they could do anything. We had no control of-- we lost total control of our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the fellow doing while they were cutting his beard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you look at his eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I look at his eyes? I looked at his eyes. They look sad, but he didn't say anything. Because he was helpless. Because it was either your life or your silence. And they started to take away to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every time that they would take my father away to work, we were sitting and trembling. Is he going to come back home? Everyday were shootings and hanging and killings and horrible things going on. Did you see someone get shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, many times. You know-- you did-- without provocation. They-- they could starve and torture and murder. You know, everything was little. It was little for them to destroy us, because it was little [INAUDIBLE] murder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember one time that you saw something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I remember, because they ask somebody to dance and to sing. You know? They ask an old man to dance and to sing. So he obeyed. He danced, and he sang. But then he said, sir, you have no God. Sir, you have no God. And he refused, so they shot. So they shot. They just did such horrible things. But this is nothing in comparison to what's happened. That's why I don't dwell too much on this. Because each time we thought it's the worst, came worst things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get beaten up by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I ever get beaten up? The only time I got beaten up, before I came concentration camp, was when I was with my mother. I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were in the house usually with small children and other-- you know, grandmother. My grandmother was really lucky. Can you imagine for a granddaughter to say that she was lucky, because she died a natural death?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died shortly after Nazi invaded Poland. I was with my mother at her bedside. And she had this [INAUDIBLE] like she had the privilege to have that Jewish burial. And when it happened, I was grief-stricken when my beloved grandmother died. I loved her dearly. We had a very close relationship. But looking back, I'm grateful that she died a natural death, because that is-- my family won't have that privilege.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there sometimes you were out on the street, and they would take you for forced labor, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, several times. One time they took us, you know, to a-- potatoes from the field, you know, to do outside work. But sometimes, you know, you were lucky. You would hide. But they took more men than they took--- they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I tried not to be too much in the street. You know, because sometimes they grab you from the street, you know. But they did it more with men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where could you go and hide, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house. No, in the house--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, if you were out on the street and you would see--

speaker: interviewee
text: No place. I couldn't hide any place. There was no place to hide. It's just-- but the hide means, like, if I wanted to avoid-- to be-- not to be seen too much, we tried to as much as we can. My mother tried to encourage us to stay in the house as much as we could-- not to be-- to be together. Because we were afraid if somebody goes out, you never know if you returned. Then we had to-- rations. And our rations were smaller than those who gave-- the Polish people were also rationed. But their rations were larger. They were allowed to do certain things that we were not allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was completely different laws for Jews and for the reason that we were supposed to be the inferior race, you know. And that label, at that time, I didn't know all the things how they viewed us. It's not till later when I experienced them and I saw. And so, anyhow, under those conditions, we lived-- Did you still have some non-Jewish friends, like [? Halinka? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Halinka ?] I saw the last time when we-- when-- chased us out from our home. It was in the middle of the night, so I didn't say goodbye to [? Halinka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she still maintain a friendship after the Germans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know where she is now if she's still alive or she's not. Because when they chased us out-- so I'll tell you about that. It was October 15, 1941 in middle of the night. That time my grandmother was fortunately no longer alive, so she escaped that. That was her liberator.

speaker: interviewer
text: The storm troopers marched into our home. And they-- the-- with guns and with rifles and with dogs. All you in the house-- in the middle of the sleep-- of middle-- of middle of the night. Everybody was sleeping, everything in panic. So what do you grab first?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to stop here for a minute. I want to just-- remember something. When we realized that people are sent and chased and everything, it seems like my parents didn't give up all the money. So I remembered something the other day when I was putting on my girdle I remembered that. In Poland my mother use to have a girdle. And it had, like, has like-- in Polish they call it a Yiddish [? fish-binder. ?] It's something like a plastic that-- to hold up the corset, so it would be stiff and would hold it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember that one time I saw my mother making-- for money-- rolls and the opening here, taking out that bone and stuffing in with money. Because we already saw that they sending people away and everything. She said, in case they send us away, we should have a little bit extra money. Because we didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, our misfortune was that we didn't know, were not aware of all  this here. My grandmother remembered a different Germany that we would live through. So anyhow, when they came in the middle of the night, my mother grabbed some of her books. She took the candlesticks, you know. And she-- whatever else. She had like little in the-- but most of all the children. And we couldn't even dress properly. And we were out. And as a child I used to play. We had-- in Polish it was called [POLISH]. [POLISH] is a castle that ages ago was somebody's actual castle living there. So they put us all there, brought us all together. And from there, they shipped us on the night of October 15, 1941 to, in Poland, a ghetto that's called Nowomiosto. In German, it's called [GERMAN]. Because they changed some of the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowomiosto is N-O-W-O, and then miosto. Miosto means city. M-I-O-S-T-O. Nowomiosto. When we came there, it was a ghetto. And we were isolated from the rest of the world. Nobody could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came there, I came just with my parents, my little brother and sister, that was all. Now, my aunt, I didn't see there. And I didn't know at the time what's happened to all of them. Because in Warsaw, once they couldn't-- once they took Warsaw, as I mentioned to you before, nobody could travel anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I never said goodbye to any of my family. The only one that I said goodbye to was my cousin Gitel in 1934 when I was eight years old when she left and had her free choice to go to Palestine, fortunately. And to my grandmother, because my mother and I was at her bedside.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I remember that she-- when she-- she was healthy all the time. She was very old, but she was healthy. And then one day, she didn't feel good. And she was in her bed. And my mother was there standing. And I came over, too. And my grandmother wanted to run off the bed. So my mother said, I think grandmother is dying. And she said something in Yiddish. [YIDDISH] It's always better before you die, like she must have felt better. And it didn't take long and she died. And I was crying, as you can imagine, and my mother. And I remember my mother said to me, [YIDDISH]. Life teaches us. Death teaches us not to take for granted life, to enjoy it. So she was the only one from all her children, from all her grandchildren that she died a natural death and that I was privileged to say goodbye to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they brought us in in the ghetto, who lived with us? They brought us a family whose name was Wiatrak. Wiatrak. You know when a mill where they make-- goes around like-- the wind blows to make it to-- when you try to mill corn and wheat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. I don't know what it's called in English. But their family name was Wiatrak, because I remember. So well, previously--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wiatrak is W-I-A-T-R-A-K, Wiatrak. So where previously was one family, but six or seven families was put in one apartment. And they didn't have any modern facilities either. So you can imagine what was going on there when this one wanted to go to the store, this one was the facilities to use-- it was extremely, extremely hard. Another thing-- you know, so many things you remember later-- is that when we were-- before we came, they made, you know, a Jewish-- they took Jewish policemen. Jewish-- it was like in the ghettos, you know. They had Jewish self-government it was almost called. But they were supposed to do what the Nazis told them. But anyhow, so we moved into this house, to this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you celebrate any of the Jewish holidays, by the way, before you went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we went to the ghetto? Not really, because first of all, we did it in hiding to the extent that we can, you know. But we couldn't make Passover anymore. What would you make it? You didn't have any food. We were watched. It wasn't-- but we still tried to observe the rituals as far as we can, you know, as far as we could. Still kept on saying the [YIDDISH] and all the things. And the [YIDDISH] the little piece. But we couldn't go on anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: It no longer was-- the last Passover that I celebrated with my family was in Passover 1939. That was the first-- the last of our whole family. After that, the family start to disintegrate, you know? Because grandmother died. We went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw my aunts from Warsaw. I never saw my aunt from-- my aunt and my family from Czarnkow. I didn't know what's happened to them at that time. After the Holocaust, I found out some things. Anyhow so when we came to the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was this from where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not very far. You know, all the shtetl in Poland was not far one from the other. But exactly how long, I was never there before, so-- and under those conditions, who was thinking how far it is?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came to the ghetto. Again, the rationing, no medical thing. But people start to help each other. Self-help, you know? For the sick and for the-- and still in hiding use to teach-- my mother still used to read me from the books.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I myself start to read. We start to go on. And they start to teach the children, you know. Because some children couldn't no longer-- you know, self-help and set up kitchens, you know, to help, and for the elderly. Still went on with the Jewish way of life to the extent it was possible under those conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the people in the apartment get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very well. The tension, the crying of the children, not-- this one needed the facilities. This one couldn't get to the kitchen, the hunger. And at that point, my parents did something that I'll never forget. One day, my mother and father said, [YIDDISH], in Yiddish. As I mentioned to you, they always spoke in Yiddish. Excuse me, please. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Children, get the few belongings you have. We're going. At that time, I started to worry whether my mother still has her senses. Because I thought to myself, where is she going? Where are we going? When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was when we were in the ghetto already. And I couldn't understand. But we were-- we loved our parents. We respected them. We obeyed them without questioning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got the few belongings, which were very few, and we left. And my mother and father took us. And they went to a place where you store corn sacks-- you know, wheat.

speaker: interviewee
text: It used to be-- in Yiddish they called [YIDDISH]. In English we call it, like, a storage room for granary-- for grain. And we moved in there. And I'll never forget what my parents said. It's enough that-- [YIDDISH]. It's enough that they-- they want to destroy us from the outside. Let's not destroy ourselves. And she couldn't take the quarreling. She was a very loving and peaceful person. They couldn't-- they couldn't take to see them bickering and screaming. And people when they're crowded together without any privacy-- and from one family so many-- and there was a dirt floor with a little window. No stove. No place. And my mother was such a creative person. She could do so many things. So she and her father-- my father went. And they got-- gots from someplace brick. And they built a stove from bricks. And that's where we cooked. And there where we still sung and read and told stories and continued to protect each other and to love each other to the extent that we could. And under those conditions, since what comes later is so much more horrible, so I'm not going to dwell so much on the ghetto. Which was horrible enough, because you saw the little children. Some parents died or were killed, and the little orphans already, children starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what was the worst thing for me under the Holocaust, besides my own suffering? Is to see people that I loved suffer. There is no greater pain than to see people that you love to starve, to torture, to be killed. This was-- this was-- such a pain that I can never forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: And see people who love you-- the parents want to protect their children. The children want to protect their-- their parents. First, they try to give everything for the children. They hardly have anything themselves. The elderly people-- the Jewish teachings, Jewish laws were still continuing under those worst conditions. And in that I saw the menschlichkeit. In that I saw the-- the teachings-- how much moral strength, moral values have. And we live like this for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go out much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? From the-- not-- not too much. Not too much. They took some people. Was a couple times-- like I said, sometimes we were took for the fields, sometimes to clean up. But not on a-- I was not taken on a regular basis, you know, like to work. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father-- tell you, it was-- I don't remember even. I don't remember exactly. Certain things, I don't know, stuck more in my memory than others. But I know nobody was allowed out from the ghetto, in from the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, I don't remember whether from the ghetto they took me to work or not. I really don't remember, [? Dave. ?] I don't-- what I don't remember, I'd rather not say at all. Certain things is like would happened now. Other things, I don't know, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get much food at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could we what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get much food at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No. Very little food. So many children-- you could see children sitting in the street and crying and begging. And we-- we started-- you know, was no medication. Living in those unsanitary conditions things become not sanitary, you know? People started to be sick. And you saw-- and the worst thing was little children were begging for a piece of bread or for something, children crying. And you couldn't help them. Couldn't help them. And I saw my little brother and sister. And I would have given them my soul that I didn't have much left to give them. So that was heartbreaking. That was very heartbreaking. [CRYING] Excuse me. [CRYING] [SOBBING] But they deliberately-- they deliberately did to the [INAUDIBLE] to destroy us, not to put us in such a condition where we couldn't help too much. And yet, people risked their life to help each other. Anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: For example.

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, they would-- children would try to smuggle-- go out from the ghetto and smuggle in some things. And like I said, sometimes even if one was a little weaker, you gave a piece of yours. In that way, you helped each other, try still to, in hiding, to teach the children who didn't have a chance to go to Hebrew schools. You know, set up little schools-- still teach them song. My parents use to say-- [YIDDISH] You have to go continue as normal as we can. And this is something that they really picked up from them. In other words, not to give in to despair, but to continue to hope, and to-- [YIDDISH]. Always they use to say-- [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, sorry that I'm speaking so much Yiddish. It's OK. But because I remember it that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, in the middle of the night, approximately a year later, it was November the 17, 1942. Again, the ghetto was liquidated. And this time they informed us we going to be resettled to a family camp with better conditions. We're going to have better sanitary. We will be all together. And we looked forward to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people know what happened when people were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No. No, I didn't-- we didn't know. At least, I didn't know anything. Actually from the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] actually from the-- when we found out, it was too late, [? David. ?] It was too late. We didn't know. If people would have known, things would have been-- maybe not that much different, because you nowhere to run. You have to-- someplace to have to run. And because they made it so that to murder Jews became legal by the state. To save a Jewish child or a Jew was illegal and punishable by death. Therefore, not too many people want to risk to do it. And to the credit of some Gentile people, there were still some who risked their life and saved some Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my family nobody was hidden. But I found out. I didn't know at the time, though. I found this out after the Holocaust when certain people survived. And I asked them, where were you? They start telling the stories. I even found out I have a girlfriend who was in a Nazi's house in Germany. But she was posing as a Gentile. She looked blonde and everything. And she somehow got papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was there. And she said her heart was breaking when she heard how they talked about the Jews. And she had to be quiet. So not everybody the Holocaust started at the same time. Not everybody experiences at the same time, because we were not the same people. So everybody's experience is different. You know, some became Partisans. Some were in the resistance. And our upbringing, we didn't want to leave our parents alone. Children didn't want to leave elders. Also, we were told to hurt somebody like us not menschlich, so how can you go kill, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, later on people started to realize that what's happening. But in the beginning, nobody knew. It was a very well kept secret. That's the whole idea, because-- excuse me-- for instance before the Holocaust, somebody comes and they wanted you to become-- apostatize. They knew-- you knew what they wanted. You could say yes or no. Sometimes [INAUDIBLE] say, although, historically they could be killed for it. But here you didn't know. Everything was a well-kept secret. This was completely unique in history, it was. Anyhow, under those conditions, horrible conditions-- and I'm not going into much of the ghetto. That alone is so much suffering that it's unbelievable to see how many people died in the ghetto of starvation, of illnesses, and the suffering of the little children and the old people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people share food much in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. Many times. Many times my parents would take and-- and I myself-- I would give to my little sister and brother. Because we thought, they're younger. And while the grandmother was alive, we always protected the grandmother first. But unfortunately she-- unfortunately and fortunately, she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, on November-- the night of November 17, 1942, the Nazis told us we're going to be resettled to a better place. And we didn't know where we going or anything. They gave us this time a little more bread, and we were so happy [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, maybe there is actually going to be a turnaround.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, and we went-- they put us aboard the train, put us in hermetically sealed cattle trains, packed us in like sardines without food in the trains, just what we got for the ration. No facilities to relieve ourselves. In the train already when we came-- we traveled from November 17. When we arrived to Auschwitz-Birkenau it was November 22nd.
